title: ASIC's D-Day looms for private credit
full_text:
The corporate cop has heard enough talk from the private credit sector. It's time for action, and D-Day is less than two weeks away. The Australian Securities and Investments Commission wants to see every player in Australia's private credit industry refresh their asset valuations for the end of the financial year on June 30. And it's not just about the numbers.
ASIC wants to see that firms have challenged valuation assumptions, tested and verified the information used to reach valuations, and followed the financial reporting audit and assurance processes this sector must demonstrate if it is to be taken seriously.
After two years of studying the sector, spotting potential problem areas - including disclosure, reporting and valuation processes - consulting with industry players and setting down principles they should follow, ASIC sees the June 30 valuation period as the last chance for private credit firms to get with the program.
"This is a line in the sand moment," ASIC commissioner Simone Constant says. "We've looked to make sure that we step up our calls to action progressively, so it's not like we're looking to take industry by surprise. We want to see private credit done well, but this is absolutely a ratchet up to a line in the sand."
In response to changing macro conditions and growing concerns over cracks in the United States private credit sector, ASIC has spent the past few months surveying 22 managers who oversee 52 funds and $76 billion of assets. Constant says a few trends are clear.
Credit conditions are deteriorating as the economy tightens and rates rise, although it is uneven and the sector's shortcomings make it hard to get a complete picture; loan amendments and impairments point to rising stress, but may not provide a fulsome picture. Higher inflation and supply disruptions are also making life tougher for some borrowers.
Investor redemption requests remain contained on a sector-wide view, but local feeder funds for the US private credit industry, which has a high exposure to software companies facing disruption from AI, are feeling the pinch.
Australia's private credit sector has very little direct exposure to software firms and is instead dominated by loans to the property industry, but this creates other risks, including concerns that some private credit lenders are far too exposed to a small group of developers.
More broadly, there are fewer new private credit funds being started in Australia, and fund raising has slowed, further tightening lending conditions. In other words, the private credit sector is in the early stages of its first real stress test in decades.
That's worrying enough. But what really concerns Constant is that the macro backdrop is exacerbating broader problems ASIC has identified across the sector.
Disclosure remains far too uneven, and there is still no sector-wide definition of what constitutes a default. Concentration risk isn't well enough understood or monitored. A weaker economy increases the potential for conflicts, the most obvious being that asset writedowns might make fundraising harder.
So much of this comes back to valuations. "We are concerned that there are pockets where valuations are lagging that economic reality," Constant says.
Take property, where Australia's sector is concentrated. An environment of high inflation, high interest rates and slowing housing activity will create a range of pressures, from cost escalation and project delays to soft pre-sales and tougher refinancing conditions. ASIC's question is simple: are those risks going to be reflected in June 30 valuations? And if not, what is the basis for that judgment?
Constant is pleased to see that work continues in the private credit sector to turn ASIC's principles into industry standards, which should help address the hodge-podge of disclosure and governance practices that currently operate in the sector.
But after a relatively measured approach towards the regulation of private credit, ASIC's patience is justifiably wearing thin.
JAMES THOMSON
j.thomson@afr.com
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: James Thomson
heading: Regulation
